perturbation.original_text: The term "Information" shall mean all information that Big Sky has furnished or is furnishing to Holdings, whether furnished before or after the date of this Agreement, whether tangible or intangible and in whatever form or medium provided, as well as all information generated by Holdings or by its Representatives, as defined below, that contains, reflects or is derived from the furnished information.
perturbation.changed_text: The term "Information" shall mean only that information clearly labeled as 'Confidential' and explicitly provided in writing by Big Sky to Holdings. Information derived from unlabeled materials, oral discussions, or Holdings' own analysis shall not be considered "Information" under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Information' is broad. This change narrows it significantly, potentially excluding information considered confidential under trade secret laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Statutes § 325C.01 - Uniform Trade Secrets Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified definition may fail to protect information that qualifies as a trade secret under Minnesota law, which doesn't require explicit labeling. MN ST § 325C.01 protects information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known, even if it is not explicitly marked as 'Confidential'. This modification adds a labeling requirement that is not found in the definition of 'trade secret'.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.01 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.01 325C.02 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.01 Version List 325C.01 DEFINITIONS. § Subdivision 1. Scope. As used in sections 325C.01 to 325C.07 , the terms defined in these sections have the meanings given them, unless the context requires otherwise. § Subd. 2. Improper means. "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. § Subd. 3. Misappropriation. "Misappropriation" means: (i) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the discloser's or user's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of the discloser's or user's position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. § Subd. 4. Person.
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly narrows the definition of 'Information' to only include explicitly labeled 'Confidential' information, which contradicts Minnesota trade secret law. MN ST § 325C.01, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, protects trade secrets even if they are not explicitly labeled as confidential, focusing on whether the information derives independent economic value from not being generally known. The modified contract language introduces a labeling requirement not present in the legal definition, thereby failing to adequately protect trade secrets under Minnesota law. The change introduces a condition (explicit labeling) that does not exist in the legal definition of a trade secret, potentially leading to the misappropriation of trade secrets without legal recourse due to the restrictive contractual definition.

perturbation.original_text: Each party acknowledges and agrees that in the event of any breach of this Agreement by it, the other would be irreparably and immediately harmed and could not be made whole by monetary damages. Accordingly, it is agreed that, in addition to any other remedy to which it may be entitled at law or in equity, each party shall be entitled to an injunction of injunctions (without the posting of any bond and without proof of actual damages) to prevent breaches or threatened breaches of this Agreement and/or to compel specific performance of this Agreement, and that neither party nor any of its Representatives will oppose the granting of such relief.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that in the event of a minor breach of this Agreement, monetary damages shall be the sole and exclusive remedy. An injunction may only be granted for breaches involving public dissemination of Information deemed by a court to be of extraordinary value.
perturbation.explanation: This modification limits the availability of injunctive relief, potentially conflicting with equitable remedies available under Minnesota law for breach of contract involving confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Statutes § 325C.02 - Injunctive Relief for Misappropriation of Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text allows injunctive relief without proving actual damages. This change contradicts Minnesota Statutes § 325C.02, which allows for injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets. The modification now requires the party to prove information is of "extraordinary value". It also creates a class of 'minor breaches' where injunction is not available. These limitations do not align with the broad equitable powers of a court to issue injunctions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.02 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.02 325C.01 325C.03 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.02 Version List 325C.02 INJUNCTIVE RELIEF. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1980 c 594 s 4 ; 1987 c 1 s 1 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly restricts the availability of injunctive relief, limiting it to only "breaches involving public dissemination of Information deemed by a court to be of extraordinary value," and classifying 'minor breaches' where injunction is not available. This directly contradicts Minnesota Statutes § 325C.02, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that "Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined." The statute does not impose the condition of 'extraordinary value' or carve out exceptions for 'minor breaches.' The original text allows for injunctive relief without needing to prove actual damages, while the changed text imposes additional hurdles, thus creating a contradiction with the law's provision for injunctive relief in misappropriation cases.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Minnesota applicable to contracts between residents of Minnesota that are to be wholly performed within such state.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law can affect the interpretation and enforcement of the contract. Minnesota and Delaware have different bodies of contract law. This can significantly alter the outcome of any disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Statutes § 336.1-301 - Uniform Commercial Code - Choice of Law
perturbation.law_explanation: The contract initially specifies Minnesota law, reflecting the parties' presumed connection to Minnesota. Changing the governing law to Delaware (without justification) potentially violates MN ST § 336.1-301 if the transaction does not bear a reasonable relation to Delaware.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 336.1-301 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 336 Section 336.1-301 336.1-209 336.1-302 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 336 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 336.1-301 Version List Recent History 2024 336.1-301 Amended 2024 c 93 art 1 s 3 2004 336.1-301 New 2004 c 162 art 1 s 16 336.1-301 TERRITORIAL APPLICABILITY; PARTIES' POWER TO CHOOSE APPLICABLE LAW. (a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), the Uniform Commercial Code applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) section 336.2-402 ; (2) sections 336.2A-105 and 336.2A-106 ; (3) section 336.4-102 ; (4) section 336.4A-507 ; (5) section 336.5-116 ; (6) section 336.8-110 ; (7) sections 336.9-301 through 336.9-307 ; (8) section 336.12-107 . History: 2004 c 162 art 1 s 16 ; 2024 c 93 art 1 s 3 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts MN ST § 336.1-301. The original contract specified Minnesota law. The modified text changes the governing law to Delaware, potentially violating MN ST § 336.1-301(a), which states that parties may agree to the law of another state if the transaction bears a "reasonable relation" to that state. The provided information offers no reasonable relation to Delaware. The change, therefore, contradicts the statute by potentially applying Delaware law inappropriately.

perturbation.original_text: During such time as the parties are involved in discussions or negotiations concerning the Transaction and for a period of at least 60 days after conclusion thereof, neither party shall engage in any transactions whatsoever regarding the common stock of the other, whether publicly traded or privately held, registered or nonregistered, including, but not limited to, buying, selling, taking or granting options in or making or receiving offers or tender offers to buy, sell, trade, or exchange any stock of the other, without the express written consent of the other and without first fully complying with all applicable securities laws and regulations and the rules of all exchanges upon which the stock of each respective party is traded, provided however, that the foregoing shall not be construed to limit the right of either party to invest in mutual funds or other commercially available investment vehicles that may acquire the stock of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: During negotiations regarding the Transaction, neither party shall engage in transactions of common stock of the other exceeding $10,000, provided they disclose such transactions to the other party within 24 hours.
perturbation.explanation: This weakens the restriction on stock transactions and creates a loophole through disclosure and a dollar limit, potentially conflicting with insider trading laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification now allows for transactions that could violate 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, which prohibits the use of manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. Even with disclosure, trading on non-public information obtained during negotiations could constitute illegal insider trading. The regulation does not specify a dollar threshold. This creates a loophole.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows for transactions up to $10,000 with disclosure, which directly contradicts the intent of 17 CFR § 240.10b-5. The original contract forbade any transactions. The law snippets, particularly Snippet 2, state that it is unlawful to "employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud" or "engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security." This regulation doesn't allow exceptions based on dollar amount or disclosure; insider trading laws prohibit using non-public information for personal gain, irrespective of the amount, creating a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.
